entry.title: SAP BDC Key Features
entry.content:
SAP BDC Key Features

In my last blog post (SAP BDC for Dummies - SAP Community) I have explained the concept of SAP BDC .

Now let us see the key features of SAP BDC.

There are 5 key features of SAP BDC.

  1. BDC Cockpit
  2. Intelligent apps
  3. Knowledge Graphs
  4. Data Products
  5. Object store

 

  1. BDC Cockpit

The BDC Cockpit in SAP Business Data Cloud (BDC) is the central user interface for managing and orchestrating all components of the BDC environment. It plays a crucial role for administrators, data scientists, and analysts by providing a unified workspace to explore, configure, and deploy data-driven solutions.

The SAP Business Data Cloud Cockpit is:

  • The main entry point for interacting with the BDC platform.
  • A centralized UI for managing the data landscape, applications, and data products.
  • A tool for exploring, deploying, and activating intelligent applications and data packages.

Key Features of the BDC Cockpit

  • Landscape Configuration
    • Set up and manage connections to SAP and non-SAP source and target systems.
    • Define which components (e.g., SAP S/4HANA, SuccessFactors) are part of your SAP BDC data landscape.
  • Artifact Management
    • Data Packages: Bundled data models and dashboards for specific scenarios.
    • Intelligent Applications: Prebuilt apps for reporting and analytics.
    • Discover and deploy: View metadata like category, system type, version, and purpose.
  • Data Product Activation
    • Activate data packages to make Data Products available via Foundation Services.
    • These products can then be used in SAP Datasphere, SAP Analytics Cloud, or AI/ML workflows.
  • User Roles
    • Administrators: Configure systems and manage access.
    • Data Scientists/Analysts: Deploy models, explore data, and build insights.

 2. Intelligent apps

 Intelligent Applications in SAP Business Data Cloud are AI-powered, adaptive applications designed to transform business operations by learning from data, understanding business context, and automating decision-making processes. 

 Each intelligent application in SAP Business Data Cloud is underpinned by curated, governed data products, sets of structured business data and metadata designed to accelerate use cases and reduce data integration overhead. And with SAP Databricks natively available in SAP Business Data Cloud, users can leverage SAP data products with existing lakehouses with a zero-copy approach.

An SAP Business Data Cloud administrator can install intelligent apps to the SAP Datasphere and SAP Analytics Cloud tenants in the SAP Business Data Cloud formation.

When an intelligent app is installed:

  • SAP-managed spaces are created in SAP Datasphere to contain the insight app content.
  • Replication flows, tables, views, and analytic models are created in these spaces to ingest, prepare and expose the required data to SAP Analytics Cloud.

 3. Knowledge Graphs

SAP Business Data Cloud Knowledge Graphs refer to a powerful capability within SAP HANA Cloud and the broader SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) that enables businesses to connect, contextualize, and analyze their enterprise data using graph-based models. 

SAP Knowledge Graphs are semantic data structures that:

  • Connect structured and unstructured data across SAP systems.
  • Model relationships between business entities (e.g., customers, products, invoices).
  • Enable AI and analytics to understand business context more deeply.
  • Support natural language queries and intelligent automation.

They are now a general availability (GA) feature in SAP HANA Cloud as of Q1 2025(What’s New in SAP HANA Cloud – March 2025 - SAP Community)

Key Features of Knowledge Graphs 

  • Ontology-Driven Modelling
    • At its core, an ontology is a formal representation of knowledge within a particular domain. It defines the types of entities that exist in that domain and the relationships between them.

                   Example: A "Vendor" might be linked to "Orders", "Invoices", and "Support Tickets".

    • Knowledge graphs use ontologies to define business concepts and relationships.
  • Multi-Model Integration
    • Works alongside relational, spatial, JSON, and vector engines in SAP HANA Cloud.
    • Enables a data fabric approach—unifying diverse data types and formats.
  • AI Enablement
    • Powers SAP Joule and other AI agents by grounding them in real business data.
    • Reduces hallucinations in AI outputs by providing context-aware answers 
  • Natural Language Access
    • Users can query data using plain language.
    • AI agents can retrieve and act on data across SAP systems.
  • Pre-Populated Graphs
    • Comes with pre-built knowledge graphs for common SAP modules (e.g., S/4HANA, SuccessFactors).

Read more about knowledge graphs in Connecting the Facts: SAP HANA Cloud’s Knowledge G... - SAP Community

4. Data Products

Data Product is a grouping concept that carries the relevant metadata to describe the dataset and its data qualities. A Data Product itself is not directly consumable, but it exposes data through output ports, which can be implemented as APIs or events. In the first iteration of BDC, the primary Data Products expose a single output port as delta-sharing API. In principle, there can be multiple output ports of various API and/or event types. To understand data ingestion, lineage, and dependencies, Data Products can also have input ports which are the equivalent of an ORD Integration Dependency that lists the APIs and events that are used to ingest or process the data.

Types of Data Products: There are three main types of Data Products:

  • SAP-managed Data Products: Pre-built by SAP, focusing on data from ERP and line-of-business applications.
  • Customer-defined Data Products: Created by customers to meet specific analytical needs, leveraging data from various sources.
  • Partner-defined Data Products: Developed by SAP partners, offering specialized datasets tailored to specific industries or use cases. 

5. Object store

The Object Store in SAP Business Data Cloud (BDC)—delivered via the SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP)—is a cloud-native storage service designed to handle unstructured data such as files, documents, images, and large binary objects (BLOBs).

This service is particularly useful for handling large amounts of unstructured data, such as documents, images, and backups.

Key Features of Object Store

  1. Instance Sharing: The Object Store on SAP BTP supports instance sharing, which allows multiple applications to share databases, messaging queues, and other services. This feature helps in reusing computing resources, exchanging data, and reducing costs.
  2. Integration with Hyperscalers: The Object Store can be integrated with various hyperscaler options such as Azure Blob Storage, Amazon S3, and Google Cloud Storage. This flexibility allows users to leverage their existing cloud infrastructure.
  3. Service Plans: The Object Store offers different service plans, with the 'standard' plan enabling the instance sharing functionality. Users need to upgrade to the 'standard' plan to utilize this feature.

 

These are key features in SAP BDC for now.

 

Happy Learning!

 

Swati Gawade-Shingade.

Cognizant technology Solutions.

entry.title: Release Navigator for SAP BTP - What's New in Q2 2025
entry.content:
The Release Navigator for SAP BTP continues to evolve and expand, offering our customers improved resources and streamlined access to product updates. As we step into Q2 2025, we are excited to announce the addition of 11 new product pages. Read more about the details in this blog post.

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

Collaborating closely with SAP BTP experts, we've expanded the release coverage by introducing 11 new product pages. These pages compile links to all available release information, such as schedules, pre- and post-release communications, blogs, and more:

  • SAP Audit Log Service
  • SAP Authorization and Trust Management Service
  • SAP BTP, Cloud Foundry environment
  • SAP Connectivity Service
  • SAP Credential Store
  • SAP Custom Domain Service
  • SAP Destination Service
  • SAP Integration Suite, Advanced Event Mesh
  • SAP Malware Scanning Service
  • SAP Private Link Service
  • SAP Secure Login Service for SAP GUI

You will notice that several of these pages share certain resources which are relevant across SAP BTP services that are relating to security.

Here's a glimpse at our new additions:

 

1. SAP Audit Log Service

This page contains specific resources for SAP Audit Log service as well as key release resources for SAP BTP security services, which also partially cover SAP Audit Log service among others. Here’s a list of what you can find on this page:

  • SAP Audit Log service
    • Release Schedule in SAP Note
    • What’s New Viewer: Release Notes
    • Documentation: SAP Help Portal
    • SAP Discovery Center
  • SAP BTP Security services
    • SAP Roadmap Explorer
    • SAP Community Page & Tag
    • Documentation: SAP Help Portal
    • SAP Security Products and Solutions Newsletter
    • Customer Influence

Check it out.

Currently, the Release Navigator for SAP BTP proudly covers content for 41 products and services. See the full list below (underlined product pages have been added this update).

  • SAP AI Core

More pages dedicated to specific products and services are expected in future updates.

 

For centralized information regarding the release of new features for SAP BTP products and services, consult the Consolidated Release Schedule for SAP BTP. Regarding the Release Navigator updates, refer to this update plan:

*Disclaimer: The schedule is subject to alterations without notice, including potential delays or the integration of changes in subsequent update windows. It doesn't establish a commitment, promise, or legal duty to deliver any material, code, or functionality. Looking into the remaining quarters of 2025, updates will focus on quarterly content maintenance and a steady extension of product coverage. No substantial UI updates are planned for the time being.

 

The Readiness@Scale team within SAP Enterprise Support is driven to ensure release readiness across the SAP Enterprise Support portfolio. Our mission is to empower SAP customers to effectively adopt and utilize innovations.

If you have feedback or specific requests for content within the release navigator, feel free to contact us.

Don't forget to explore our team’s blogs tagged under Readiness@Scale.

Happy navigating!

Readiness@Scale  
SAP Enterprise Support

entry.title: Export Datasphere Views Data in CSV Format
entry.content:
Out of many few of us think Datasphere itself is a Database because datasphere itself has local Tables but how many of us know there will be Hana database behind Datasphere screen   

Many of us faced this situation during our test process we need data to  export  that are in Graphical views into CSV format and we can't do it until we create Analytical model and frame it by connecting with AO, Here I wanted to show how to export data from a View in CSV .

 

#2 Click on Database Access à Click on Database Analysis Users 

 

 

Check with your Admin to create DB Access Users and its not Space specific 

Open Database Explorer

Here I considered Graphical View with text semantics for my example ( You can export or run Analytical model as well) 

You can see the definition of view in Hana Database

 

You can see the definition of view in Hana Database

 

Click on SQL if you want to write any custom SQL 

 

Then Run

 

Click on Download icon

Your Dataset is in your Downloads folder 🍾

I know the blog is simple but would like to share my knowledge for some one who is looking for this grey area.

Incase if you are struggling to export more than 1000 rows then you can change max number of row from the below setting 

 

Regards,

Kartheek

I should thank to @somenpodder who gave me this opportunity to explore many more opportunities behind the datasphere.

entry.title: SAP Takes Center Stage as Visionary Sponsor at the Databricks Summit
entry.content:
SAP is gearing up for Databricks Data + AI Summit 2025, happening June 9-12 in San Francisco, California — and we’re proud to join as a Visionary Sponsor. This premier gathering of data and AI leaders brings together thousands of executives, engineers, scientists, and architects, who are redefining the convergence of data and AI in future innovations.

At the heart of our presence this year is SAP Business Data Cloud, our next generation data and analytics solution that unifies and governs all SAP data and seamlessly connects with third-party data.  As a fully managed SaaS solution, it brings together SAP Datasphere, SAP Analytics Cloud, and SAP Business Warehouse with a unified experience that delivers transformational insights across all lines of business.

A major highlight: SAP’s landmark partnership with Databricks.  With SAP Databricks, now a core component of SAP Business Data Cloud, you can bring the power of Databricks Data Intelligence Platform natively into your SAP data landscape.  This means data teams can now unite mission-critical data with industry-leading AI, machine learning, data science, and data engineering capabilities—all within a governed, enterprise-grade environment.

Join us at Data + AI Summit to experience SAP Business Data Cloud, including SAP Databricks, in action to amplify the value of AI with your most powerful business data. 

We invite you to attend our hosted sessions, visit us on the show floor at booth #E518, and schedule an in-person meeting with our team of experts to discuss your data strategy and AI goals.

Attend SAP-hosted session: 

Here are three sessions you won’t want to miss!

SAP and Databricks Open a Bold New Era of Data and AI​

Tuesday, June 10 at 12:20pm PST | Presenter: Haridas Nair, Global Head of Ecosystem Integrations

SAP and Databricks have formed a landmark partnership that brings together SAP's deep expertise in mission-critical business processes and semantically rich data with Databricks' industry-leading capabilities in AI, machine learning, and advanced data engineering. From curated, SAP-managed data products to zero-copy Delta Sharing integration, discover how SAP Business Data Cloud empowers data and AI professionals to build AI solutions that unlock unparalleled business insights using trusted business data.

From Overwhelmed to Empowered: How SAP is Democratizing Data & AI with Databricks to Solve Problems

Tuesday, June 10, at 1:10pm PST | Presenter: Sabrina Childress, Technology & Innovation Manager

Scaling the adoption of Data & AI within enterprises is critical for driving transformative business outcomes. Learn how the SAP Experience Garage, SAP’s largest internal enablement and innovation driver, is turning all employees into data enthusiast through the integration of Databricks technologies. The SAP Experience Garage platform brings together colleagues with various levels of data knowledge and skills in one seamless space. Here, they can explore and use tangible datasets and data science/AI tooling from Databricks, enablement capabilities, and collaborative features to tackle real business-related challenges and create prototypes that find their way into SAP’s ecosystem.

SAP Business Data Cloud: Fuel AI with SAP data products across ERP and lines-of-business

Wednesday, June 11, at 11:30am PST | Presenter: Niclas Schlautkoetter, Chief Technology Architect

Unlock the power of your SAP data with SAP Business Data Cloud—a fully managed SaaS solution that unifies and governs all SAP data while seamlessly connecting it with third-party data. As part of SAP Business Data Cloud, SAP Databricks brings together trusted, semantically rich business data with industry-leading capabilities in AI, machine learning, and data engineering. Discover how to access curated SAP data products across critical business processes, enrich and harmonize your data without data copies using Delta Sharing, and leverage the results across your business data fabric. See it all in action with a demonstration.

Meet with SAP at the Summit:

Visit us on the show floor at booth #E518, located in Hall E of the Moscone Center to connect with SAP experts and learn how SAP can help you unlock the full value of data across your organization to accelerate your data and AI workloads.

Want to connect 1:1 with SAP? Schedule an in-person meeting during the event using our meeting scheduler, and be invited to our exclusive evening reception.

To learn more about SAP at Databricks Data + AI Summit, click here.

We look forward to seeing you at Data + AI Summit!

entry.title: ARIBA API data extraction & reporting with SAP BTP
entry.content:
Introduction

In this blog post I'm sharing some highlights from a recent project work involving data extraction from ARIBA API into SAP Datasphere OpenSQL schema using Integration suite with a basic dashboard in Analytics cloud. 

        ✅ Use Integration Suite iflow to extract data from ARIBA API.

        ✅ Persisting records in Datasphere OpenSQL schema

        ✅ Data modelling in Datasphere

        ✅ Reporting in Analytics Cloud

Upcoming Functionality with SAP BDC 🚀

With the annoucement of SAP Business Data cloud (BDC), a fully managed SaaS solution that unifies and governs all SAP data and seamlessly connects with third-party data, we will soon have standard Data Products & Insight Applications across SAP LoB solutions, and with that we will have direct access to such data assets without the need for data integration as in this blog post. SAP has already started publishing these data products and these can be looked up here : SAP Data Products.

In this post you get to see some highlights with screenshots on how SAP Integration suite can work with ARIBA APIs, with end to end data modelling and visualization. Its quite relevant for customers with exisitng subscription of Integration Suite. 

SAP Discovery Mission 🔍

For detailed implemenation of ARIBA API spend data extraction please refer this SAP Discovery mission which comes with Integration Suite iflows, sample SQL scripts to execute on Datasphere etc. 

While the discovery mission is very detailed , we will observe in the blog post the overall flow with sample implementation steps using a single iflow and I hope it will help understand the overall flow when modelling ARIBA apis for extraction. 

ARIBA API ⛓

I have used the Purchase orders buyer API which can tested using the free sandbox at api.sap.com 

ARIBA Network Purchase Orders buyer API

Prepackaged Integration Content 🎁

With Integration Suites prepackaged content you get well designed iflows which you can configure for your specific systems & apis.

Explore Prepakaged Content on Business Accelerator Hub .

For the integration in discussion as well we have the standard iflows listed as below:

ARIBA connection iflows on Business Accelerator Hub

On your integration suite tenant you can access this prepackaged content under Discover --> integrations 

Published ARIBA connection package in Integration suite : 

As part of this packages you get 2 iflows as described below : 

MODIFIED INTEGRATION FLOW (iflow): ↖️

I did some modifications on this prepackaged iflow so that I can connect to ARIBA and then process data using below addtional operators : 

  • General Splitter
  • Content modifier
  • JDBC receiver (OpenSQL schema of SAP Datasphere) 

If needed you can download this from my Github page : Download modified iflow

Here is a screenshot of the modified iflow : 

So the second iflow "output_from_ARIBA" is simply calling the first iflow using ProcessDirect and it gets deployed with an HTTP end point, which when queried triggers the first iflow "Connect SAP ARIBA to the SAP ARIBA APIs". The iflows are used to just show the mechanism of fast and direct communication between iflows using Process Direct, you can read more it here : ProcessDirect 

In the screenshot you see that "output_from_ARIBA" iflow gets deployed with an address of /vinay/Ariba, this will reflect in the eventual endpoint when the iflow is deployed. 

 

Below we see the processDirect Adapter making an internal call to the first iflow, which gets deployed with an address : /Ariba/OpenAPI.

 

Extract data from ARIBA API ▶️

Lets now look at the first iflow "connect SAP Ariba to the SAP Ariba APIs" in detail. We will go through each important section of the iflow and understand the processing with screen shots. 

GET ACCESS TOKEN

The first step in the iflow execution is to get an Access token from ARIBA's Oauth URL with encoded client id/secret, an auth token is returned which is then saved in the header.

 

 

GET DOCUMENT

Now when then access token is retrieved we can further add additional headers for our subsequent call to ARIBA's Purchase order buyer API, e.g in this case the ARIBA networkx- ID along with the API key. 

Now we make the final HTTP request to ARIBA API, in the query ARIBA Realm and filters can also be specified. 

JSON TO XML Converter

The JSON structure of the API was intitially tested at the api.sap.com, and now we have used JSON to XML converter, so the message can be parsed using the XPATH - which is a query language to navigate through elements and attributes in an XML document.

GENERAL SPLITTER

Splitter will simply split the XML message based on the number of records as in this example there will be multiple purchase orders returned from the ARIBA API. 

CONTENT MODIFIER

In the message body of the Content Modifier, we now use Type Expression, to build a SQL query using the exchange properties we desgined above. 

Finally, we reach the point of the database call which is configured as the OpenSQL schema of the Datasphere. 

The JDBC connection is maintained in the integration suite tenant as below : 

Runnning the Integration Flow  

We deploy both iflows and we can see the Endpoint against the iflow below : 

Call the endpoint either using Postman or visual Studio code and you will see the message processed as below : 

We now verify the OpenSQL table to see the persisted records : ( Please note we only had a few records from the sabdbox API used in this case and the iflow was run mutiple times so you see some duplicate records below)

 

 

Data modelling in Datasphere 📈 📊

With the data moved to Datasphere OpenSQL schema we can access the table in Datasphere Data Builder, Open a new Graphical view and from the left navigation under sources you would see the linked openSQL Schema. Drag & drop the table on the canvas. I have added a calculated column here to convert the POAMOUNT to a decimal value ( it was nvarchar after the initial data import). Save and deploy the view.

 

Create an Analytical model using the Graphical view created above, use the POAMOUNT field as a Measure : 

Reporting in SAP Analytics Cloud 📊

I'm using a linked SAP analytics cloud tenant, you can learn how to connect Datasphere & Analytics cloud tenants here.

Once linked, open a black story and proceed to add new data --> Existing Data model --> select Datasphere system from the left navgation as below.

Select your deployed model under the datasphere space name and build a story with data that you processed using the iflow : 

 

CONCLUSION 🔚

Hope this helps the community members to get an overall idea of the integration discussed in this blog post and sparks ideas for your exisitng or future projects. 

Thank you for your time & attention! 

APPENDIX 

Monitoring the iflow and the message flow using trace

As some of you must already know Integration suite provides several advanced capabilities to monitor the iflow. In the below steps we will enable the trace on the main iFlow and see how different components are workign together : 

Enabling the Trace : 

Navigate to Monitor --> Integrations and APIs --> Manage Integration Content and look for the "Started" iflows for ARIBA.

Here we enable the Trace by changing the "Log Level" to trace. The trace only gets enabled for 10 mins (default setting) as Integration suite startes storing on the messages. 

Trigger the iFlow again to fetch the records and so that we can now see the tracing results : 

Goto : Monitor --> Integrations and APIs --> Monitor Message Processing 

you can now see the trace as below, click on the "Trace" :

 

Now, we can easily check various parameters, headers, exchange properties & payloads across various the iflow: 

You can see the JSON payload coming through below : 

Splitting & XML conversion happens in the iflow and here we see the splitted payload: 

Extracted Exchange properties can be seen here (e.g documentNumber) : 

Finally, we see the prepared SQL statement.

Tracing is a very powerful tool to debug the various phases of the iflow and at the same time allowing us to format the data passing through to make required corrections.

entry.title: Consuming data from SAP Datasphere in SAP BTP CAP Applications
entry.content:
Premise:

Often, an application developed on SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) requires data from various systems for different purposes. SAP Datasphere, which unifies and integrates all SAP and non-SAP data from various sources, is an excellent resource for such business data. In a recent project with one of the leading banks in Europe, we observed that machine learning experts preferred working directly with the underlying data using Python for building and training models. However, since the SAP Cloud Application Programming (CAP) Model does not natively support Python, it's important to consider alternative approaches that allow integration with Python-based workflows and thus bridging the gap.

In the context of an enterprise, imagine a large business with stringent internal and external requirements for data quality, ensuring the reliability of any form of data that is processed, particularly if the documents are related to financial accounting, as these are very critical. Considering table in S/4HANA, which combines multiple financial journals into one consolidated table and holds all the financial data, has high volume and complexity that can give rise to data quality issues. These issues can be costly when discovered at later stages of accounting-related processes, posing significant challenges to the enterprise. Given the high complexity and variety in accounting scenarios, along with significant data volume across multiple business divisions, user data quality control measures must be complemented by machine-enabled solutions. Machine learning algorithms can play a crucial role in supporting responsible organizational units by detecting potential data quality issues early and assisting in their resolution. This proactive approach helps maintain data quality throughout the entire accounting process.

The accounting ledger line-item data from the S/4HANA application can be sourced into SAP Datasphere to perform anomaly detection using machine learning, ensuring no additional load on the operational S/4 system. The anomaly detection algorithm identifies potential anomalies across document line items, which are then presented to end users, such as controllers, through SAP Analytics Cloud. To assist in investigating detected anomalies, business users like controllers can be provided with the reasoning behind why the machine learning algorithm flagged the relevant items as unusual.

Such an application can be easily realised by following the detailed steps outlined in the blog, Hands-on Tutorial: Machine Learning with SAP Datasphere. The blog also includes a hands-on code repository providing snippets to get you started.

Developing this concept further, an application developed on SAP BTP can interact seamlessly with the anomaly detection outcomes in natural language. Additionally, such an application can integrate with the S/4HANA system and could automate the creation of corrective actions, ensuring complete and streamlined workflow.

Solution:

The application on BTP is built following steps detailed in the reference architecture, Retrieval Augmented Generation and Generative AI on SAP BTP and the associated mission, GenAI Mail Insights: Develop a CAP application using GenAI and Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG). The mission includes associated code on GitHub, btp-cap-genai-rag.

Although the provided code isn’t an exact match, it offers all the necessary elements in the code and can be easily adjusted based on your needs, such as here for building an application to interact with anomaly detection outcomes. One key difference for our scenario is to be able to refer or integrate with the data source (tables) in SAP Datasphere within the SAP Cloud Application Programming Model (CAP) framework. The following two options are available for this integration.

SAP HANA Cross Container Access:

SAP CAP,  being an opinionated framework, provides instructions on how to use databases with CAP applications. SAP HANA Cloud is the recommended and standard database for productive usage. Deploying to SAP HANA from SAP CAP is done using SAP HANA Deployment Infrastructure (HDI). The SAP HANA Deployment Infrastructure (HDI) uses containers to store design-time artifacts and the corresponding deployed run-time (catalog) objects. 

Prepare SAP Datasphere space by enabling access to HDI containers. This process is used to create a mapping between SAP Datasphere and the SAP BTP sub account, thereby enabling bi-directional access. In one direction, to access the artefacts from HDI containers associated with the corresponding SAP HANA Cloud attached to one of your SAP BTP sub accounts in the SAP Datasphere spaces. In another direction, it allows the SAP HANA Cloud underneath the SAP Datasphere to also deploy HDI containers to be used for SAP CAP applications.

In SAP Datasphere space where the anomaly detections results are made, create a technical database user to expose the artefacts within the space for consumption as source for the CAP application. This database user must have appropriate privileges and should be enabled for HDI consumption.

Now, SAP HANA cross container access can be used to access data from one schema or HDI container by another. This blog details cross container access scenarios using user-provided service on cloud foundry.

The diagram below puts all these steps into perspective.

                                                 

To summarise, the SAP CAP application deploys an HDI container (denoted as consumer in the above diagram) on the SAP HANA Cloud underneath SAP Datasphere thereby enabling cross container access with the provider schema or HDI container (denoted as provider in the above diagram) of the corresponding SAP Datasphere space by defining synonyms and a user provided service. Once this is established, the data is brought into SAP CAP by defining @cds.persistence.exists for the necessary entities in the model.

The primary strategy in this option involves utilizing SAP HANA Cloud within SAP Datasphere for deploying the HDI container in the SAP CAP application. Should this option be unsuitable due to the SAP HANA Cloud being managed by SAP, an alternative approach detailed below may be considered.

SAP HANA Virtual Tables and Cross Container Access:

SAP HANA smart data access let's you access remote data as if the data were stored in local tables in SAP HANA, without copying the data into SAP HANA. In SAP HANA, linked databases or create virtual tables are used, which point to remote tables in different remote databases, and then SQL queries can be used in SAP HANA to access these virtual tables. In this option, you will need an SAP HANA Cloud instance for the CAP application. The virtual tables using smart data access will be created here, thereby linking the remote data source/tables to the space from SAP HANA Cloud underneath the SAP Datasphere.

Like option 1, In SAP Datasphere space where the anomaly detection’s results are made, create a technical database user to expose the artefacts within the space for consumption as source for the CAP application. This database user must have appropriate privileges and should be enabled for HDI consumption.

Once the access is enabled, create necessary virtual tables as described in the initial part of the blog Step by Step guide to creating remote data in HDI.

The diagram below puts all these steps into perspective.

Now, similar to option 1, the virtual tables can be used in the HDI container managed by the SAP CAP application using user-provided service on cloud foundry. The second part of the blog referred to in the previous para also details this.

Summary:

To summarise, SAP Datasphere is utilised to load business data, such as financial accounting information from S/4 HANA, and perform anomaly detections before writing back the data. Subsequently, a second application is developed using SAP CAP on SAP BTP to access these results and provide an interface that interacts with natural language using SAP AI Core. In effect, this approach bridges the gap between leveraging SAP Datasphere for centralised data processing and anomaly detection using Python and integrating the results seamlessly into a user-facing application built with SAP CAP on BTP.

entry.title: Configuration of Cloud Transport Management Service (CTMS) on SAP BTP for SAP Datasphere Tenants
entry.content:
SAP Datasphere is increasingly adopted in multi-tier landscapes (Dev, QA, Prod), where managing the lifecycle of analytics content in a controlled and traceable manner becomes essential. SAP Cloud Transport Management Service (CTMS) addresses this need by enabling secure, seamless, and centralized transport of artifacts across SAP Datasphere tenants through SAP BTP.

CTMS is now the recommended approach moving forward while transporting business-critical data from one Datasphere Tenant to another, as it provides complete data governance and logs while transporting objects. 

In this blog, we’ll guide you step-by-step through the configuration and setup of CTMS for Datasphere.




Prerequisites

  • BTP Global Account Admin Access

  • SAP Datasphere DW Admin Access

  • CTMS enabled on the BTP Global Account

Step 1: Enable CTMS in Your BTP Subaccount

  1. Go to SAP BTP Cockpit → Global Account → Entity Assignments.

  2. Select the subaccount for CTMS. (Usually your Dev Datasphere subaccount, You need to configure CTMS only on one subaccount where your Datasphere source account is.)

  3. Click Edit → Add Service Plan

     

Step 2: Subscribe to CTMS and Create an Instance

  1. Add a Service Plan → Cloud Transport Management → Choose the Appropriate Plans


     

  2. In the subaccount, go to Instances and Subscriptions → Create.

  3. Search for cloud transport management, and create a subscription.

     

Download the generated JSON key after creation.

 

 

Please Note: If the key is not available/not visible, Go inside the CTMS configuration menu --> Create a new service key --> Just give a name to the key and hit create.  

 

 

Step 3: Connect CTMS with SAP Datasphere

  1. Log in to your SAP Datasphere tenant.

  2. Navigate to Transport → Export.

  3. Select the My Content folder.

  4. Click on Settings.

  5. Choose Add New System.

  6. Upload the JSON key file you downloaded during the CTMS instance creation in BTP.


Step 4a: Create OAuth Client in SAP Datasphere

  1. Log in to your SAP Datasphere tenant.

  2. Go to System → Administrator → App Integration.

  3. Click on Add OAuth Client.

  4. Fill in the following:

    • Purpose: API Access

    • Access: Analytics Content Network Interaction

    • Authorization Grant Type: Client Credentials

  5. Save the Client ID and Client Secret.
    🔒Note: The client secret is shown only once. Store it securely for later use.

Step 4b: Map SAP Datasphere Tenant in BTP (Create Destination)

  1. Go to your SAP BTP Cockpit.

  2. Navigate to Connectivity → Destinations → Create Destination.

  3. Enter the following destination details:
  • URL: (Format) https:// <DSP Tenant>/api/v1/content/deploy/
  • Authentication: OAuth2ClientCredentials
  • Client ID: You can find this ID from the OAuth Client you created in the previous step. (In Datasphere)


  • Client Secret: When you create an OAuth Client in Datasphere, you get an option to view the client key. Note: Client secret is only visible once while creating an OAuth Client, make sure you take note of it. Otherwise, you need to create a new OAuth client to get a new key.
  • Token Service URL:(Format) <Token URL> ?grant_type=client_credentials. 
    The token URL can be found in the Datasphere -> App Integration section.
  • Repeat this step for each SAP Datasphere tenant (Dev, QA, Prod) in your landscape.

    Important Note: Customer needs to add the destination of each datasphere tenant they have. For Example, if the customer is using a 3-tier DSP landscape, and they want a transport layer like (Dev -> QA -> Prod) in these cases we need to add a destination for each DSP tenant

Step 5: Logging into the CTMS Portal:

When you logging into Ctms for the 1st time, it says a pop-up saying you don't have authorization. You need to log out from ctms manually and log in again. If It still says Authorization missing, please refer to the security section of the blog. 

Step 6: Define CTMS Landscape and Routes

  1. Open CTMS Cockpit → Landscape Wizard.

  2. Select a landscape template (e.g., Three-Node Landscape).

  3. For each node:

    • Name: SAP recommends having a proper naming convention in order to understand nodes travelling from one to another. 

    • Allow Upload: Only on the Dev/source node.

    • Forward Mode: Auto or Manual. 

      • Auto: This would allow you to forward the uploaded package to the next Node automatically.
      • Manual: Users need to trigger the forwarding of uploaded packages to the next Node.

    • Content-Type: Leave blank for Dev (if it doesn't allow the blank value, you can use Application Content); use Application Content for QA and Prod.

    • Destination: Choose the appropriate BTP destination. Please Note: The destination here would be from CTMS to your Datasphere tenant. 

Note: Route 1 shows the data will travel from Node 1(Dev)  --> Node 2 (QA) and Route 2 shows data will travel from Node 2 (QA) --> Node 3 (Prod)

 

Step 7: Transporting Packages

  1. In Datasphere Dev tenant, go to Transport → Export → select a package.

  2. Choose Upload to SAP Cloud Transport Management node.

  3. Select Node 2 (QA).

  4. Click Upload.

  5. In the CTMS portal, You can go to Transport Request and click on the Import All tab to import the package manually. 

    Note: CTMS will directly transport the objects to the target tenant. For example, if you have a local table 'sales' inside a space called 'finance', in the destination datasphere tenant, once the transport is succeeded, you will see the sales table in the available directly in finance space in the target system. 

 
Authorization:

Roles required for getting full access to CTMS features:

  • Administrator
  • Landscape Operator
  • Transport Operator
  • Import Operator
  • Export Operator

In addition to the above roles, if a user wants to have the rights to delete, import transport automatically, disable, or remove; the user needs to create an additional role in SAP BTP: 

  1. Go to BTP -> Roles -> Transport Operator -> Create Role

     

  2. Give the Role Name -> Source Static -> Enter the Node Value.
    Note: This node value should be the same which we created in Step 6 (Transport Landscape Wizard). Click on Enter after every node is entered. 


  3. In the Next Step -> Select 'Transport Operator'  in the Role Collection.
  4. Make sure Transport Operator TMS is added to your administrator role collection as well.

Conclusion: This guide sets up a complete CTMS workflow for SAP Datasphere across a typical Dev → QA → Prod landscape using SAP BTP. You can now securely and efficiently transport your content, manage lifecycle, and reduce manual errors.

SAP Datasphere SAP Cloud Transport Management @IanDempsey

entry.title: Health Monitoring in SAP Datasphere
entry.content:
Efficient Monitoring in SAP Datasphere: Why You Need More Than the Standard Tools

SAP Datasphere allows users to quickly and intuitively develop even complex data models. That’s one of the key reasons why many customers rely on it for larger-scale self-service analytics. However, as usage increases, it becomes critically important to manage available resources efficiently and to identify problematic data models, queries, and SAC stories early.

For effective platform operations in SAP Datasphere, ongoing monitoring of resource consumption and continuous optimization of developments are essential. In numerous customer conversations, we’ve seen how a lack of appropriate monitoring can lead to severe system issues—slow performance, failing queries, or error messages like “Database is busy”.

In this blog post, I’ll address the following key questions:

  • What metrics are most important for monitoring SAP Datasphere?

  • What standard monitoring capabilities does SAP Datasphere offer?

  • What challenges do users face when relying solely on the standard tools?

  • What is the ISR Monitoring Solution?

  • What advantages does the ISR solution offer for efficient and proactive monitoring?


Which Key Metrics Matter in SAP Datasphere Monitoring?

Effective monitoring of SAP Datasphere relies on tracking the right performance indicators. Among the most important KPIs are:

  • CPU utilization

  • Memory usage

  • Number of active threads

These are highlighted in blue on the left side of Figure above.

What influences these KPIs?

On the right side of the diagram, you'll find the key drivers that directly impact system performance, including:

  • Expensive Statements: Queries that consume above-average system resources.

  • MDS Statements from SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC): Queries sent from SAC to SAP Datasphere.

  • Executed Tasks: Scheduled tasks and jobs within the system.

Additional critical events to monitor:

  • Admission Control Events: These occur when queries are delayed or rejected due to high system load.

  • Out of Memory Events: Situations where the system lacks sufficient memory to execute a query or task.

Monitoring these KPIs and events provides a solid foundation for identifying performance bottlenecks early and maintaining a stable SAP Datasphere environment.

What Standard Monitoring Capabilities Does SAP Datasphere Offer?

SAP Datasphere provides several standard tools and methods to monitor system health and performance. However, these often require manual effort and custom SQL to get meaningful insights.

How Can Key Metrics Be Measured?

  • CPU, Memory, and Threads
    These metrics can be monitored via the SAP HANA Cockpit or by using a DB analysis user to execute custom SQL queries on the tables:

    • M_LOAD_HISTORY_HOST

    • HOST_LOAD_HISTORY_HOST

  • Expensive Statements
    These can be identified by querying the table:

    • M_EXPENSIVE_STATEMENTS
      This also requires custom SQL code and a DB analysis user.

  • MDS Statements (from SAP Analytics Cloud)
    These are only partially visible through the standard SAP Datasphere System Monitoring. For more detailed analysis, the following tables can be queried:

    • M_MULTIDIMENSIONAL_STATEMENTS

    • M_MULTIDIMENSIONAL_STATEMENT_STATISTICS

  • Task Management
    Monitoring of tasks is available per space through the Integration Monitor. For a cross-space overview, you’ll need to run custom SQL queries (via a DB analysis user) on the:

    • TASK_LOGS 

While these standard tools and tables offer access to relevant data, they often require advanced knowledge and manual query work. In practice, this limits their usefulness for proactive, scalable monitoring. Beside the DB Analysis User Option some tables could also be integrated in a Datasphere Space by activation of monitoring views in the Configuration.
(Cloud ALM is not considered here)

What Are the Challenges of Monitoring with Standard SAP Tools?

The standard tools provided by SAP Datasphere offer only limited monitoring capabilities. First and foremost, there is no single integrated monitoring solution. Instead, administrators must navigate multiple entry points such as:

  • System Monitoring

  • Integration Monitoring

  • SAP HANA Cockpit

This fragmented approach means system admins constantly switch between tools to gain a full picture.

Limited Functionality and Lack of Root Cause Analysis

Even when using all available tools, the built-in features provide limited functionality. For example, it's very difficult—if not impossible—to conduct a root cause analysis like:

"What caused the high CPU load this morning?"

Critical components such as Expensive Statements are missing or incomplete. Performing meaningful analysis often requires custom SQL code, which typically must be executed by a DB analysis user. Moreover, key data points (like Expensive Statements) are not historically logged, making it difficult to trace performance issues over time and forcing users to build additional custom solutions.

High Technical Overhead

A deep level of technical expertise is required to identify and optimize resource-intensive processes. Monitoring often feels like assembling a complex puzzle from scratch every time—costly, time-consuming, and prone to error.

The Need for an Advanced Monitoring Solution

To monitor SAP Datasphere effectively, custom extensions are almost always necessary, as the standard tools fall short of delivering the full picture. However, this approach increases both workload and risk. There is a clear need for advanced, automated monitoring solutions that provide comprehensive, efficient, and proactive system oversight.

Health Monitoring by ISR Information Products AG for SAP Datasphere

At ISR Information Products AG, we have developed an integrated and automated monitoring solution tailored specifically for SAP Datasphere.

At the heart of this solution is the Health Monitoring Cockpit—a central dashboard built in SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC). It visualizes the most important KPIs in real time and enables in-depth analysis of system performance and resource usage.

This cockpit provides a comprehensive, user-friendly overview of your Datasphere environment and helps both platform teams and developers quickly identify and resolve performance issues.

Centralized Dashboard with Integrated KPI Analysis

Our Health Monitoring solution offers a comprehensive dashboard that brings together all critical monitoring KPIs in one place. Built on a standardized data model, it correlates key metrics and influencing factors, enabling users to recognize trends and perform root cause analysis efficiently.

Key Features at a Glance:

  • User-friendly dashboard
    Intuitive interface for both technical and business users, no SQL knowledge required.

  • Integrated KPI analysis
    All relevant metrics—CPU, memory, threads, task load, query performance—presented in one consolidated view.

  • Deep-dive capabilities
    Drill-down into statement-level details, including expensive queries and SAC-driven activity, to uncover root causes of performance issues.

This approach allows platform teams to shift from reactive monitoring to proactive performance management, significantly reducing time spent on troubleshooting.

Identifying Root Causes

 

One of the key strengths of the ISR Health Monitoring solution is its ability to visually correlate system KPIs with their underlying drivers. For example, a spike in CPU usage can be analyzed in direct relation to influencing factors such as MDS statements triggered by SAP Analytics Cloud.

Root Cause Analysis – Made Visual and Actionable

  • Visual correlation between key KPIs (e.g., high CPU load) and potential causes (e.g., specific SAC queries or data models).

  • Drill-down analysis using the SAP Data Analyzer to pinpoint the root cause—such as a specific story execution—that led to the performance issue.

This capability turns raw system data into actionable insights, helping platform teams and developers address performance issues before they impact end users.

Trend Analysis

Stay on top of how your SAP Datasphere environment evolves over time. Our monitoring solution enables you to identify long-term trends, such as increasing memory consumption, recurring query spikes, or system load patterns.

You remain in full control of the data history—flexibly define how long historical data should be retained in the model to suit your operational and compliance needs.

Benefits of Trend Analysis:

  • Spot performance degradation early

  • Detect inefficient developments or increasing resource demands

  • Support capacity planning and proactive optimization

With this, trend monitoring becomes a key part of your platform strategy—not just reactive, but forward-looking.

Task Management

 

Gain a comprehensive overview of all running tasks within your SAP Datasphere system. Our solution makes it easy to identify the most resource-intensive tasks based on memory and CPU consumption.

Thanks to intuitive navigation, you can access detailed task-level insights with just a few clicks—supporting efficient, targeted performance monitoring.

Key Benefits:

  • Full visibility into scheduled and active tasks

  • Quick identification of performance bottlenecks

  • Drill-down analysis for deeper optimization

This ensures your operations team can act quickly and precisely—keeping your system stable and responsive.

Spot Resource Hogs – Identify Expensive Objects Instantly

 

Our monitoring solution automatically analyzes your SAP Datasphere environment and highlights the most resource-intensive objects—at a glance.

This allows you to quickly see which objects require optimization before they lead to high memory or CPU usage.

Why It Matters:

  • Prevent performance issues before they impact users

  • Focus on the true cost drivers in your system

  • Optimize proactively rather than reactively

It’s a powerful tool for ensuring your system runs smoothly—even as complexity and usage grow.

What Advantages Do ISR Solution Offer?

Our monitoring solution is an integrated, automated system that delivers many benefits:

Significant Reduction of Complexity

Our monitoring data model includes extensive SQL logic—such as parsing JSON-based MDS statements and linking them with CPU utilization—and standardizations like unifying CPU metrics. These features greatly simplify the complexity for users of our data model and cockpit, abstracting away technical details to provide clear, actionable insights.

Reduction of Manual Effort

Comprehensive monitoring of SAP Datasphere typically requires significant manual effort—switching between multiple UI screens (e.g., HANA Cockpit) and running custom SQL scripts for analysis. The ISR monitoring solution automates many manual tasks, such as searching for resource hogs, drastically reducing time and effort.

Centralized Monitoring Cockpit

Our solution provides a central cockpit for SAP Datasphere monitoring, summarizing all key information in one place. Access is controlled by space-level authorizations, enabling decentralized use—for example, by different business units within a self-service architecture.

Once resource-intensive objects are identified, users can perform deep-dive analyses down to individual SQL statements using the SAP Analytics Cloud Data Analyzer.

Integrated View of KPIs and Influencing Factors

The solution links key platform KPIs (such as CPU utilization) with their drivers (like executed tasks, MDS/SAC requests, and expensive statements). This connection enables rapid identification of resource-heavy developments and provides guidance for ongoing optimization of data models and SAC stories.

If you have further questions, please contact me (e.g. via LinkedIn). You want to see the solution in action -> See a webinar replay (only in german)

entry.title: Unlocking Data Potential: What I Learned from SAP Sapphire
entry.content:
Unlocking Data Potential: What I Learned from SAP Sapphire

Hi everyone! I’m Kara Reed, Senior Director of Product Marketing at SAP. At this year’s SAP Sapphire, I had the privilege of co-presenting a session with Brian Suk from Databricks on a topic that’s very close to my heart: how organizations can unlock the full value of their data through the combined power of SAP Business Data Cloud and Databricks.

Our session—" SAP and Databricks open a bold new era of data and AI"—highlighted how businesses can build trusted, AI-powered applications with SAP Databricks, a fully SAP-managed solution that combines industry leading AI/ML, data science, and data engineering capabilities with semantically rich, business-ready data from SAP applications. Let me take you behind the scenes and share a few insights from the session—and what I took away from the event overall.

Driving Value with Data + AI

Our goal was to help attendees answer three key questions:

  • Why SAP Business Data Cloud and Databricks?
  • What value can this bring to my organization?
  • How do they work together in practice?

It’s no secret that data and AI are reshaping the future of business. Companies that can turn their data into actionable insights—quickly, securely, and at scale—are the ones that will lead in their industries. But too many are still held back by siloed data, inconsistent quality, and tools that don’t talk to each other.

The Trust Gap is Real

One of the most striking stats we shared was from a recent survey of 1,200 business and IT leaders: only 34% have high trust in their data capabilities. That’s a wake-up call. Organizations are investing heavily in data platforms, yet many still struggle with aligning IT and business needs or ensuring data is ready for AI-driven use cases.

What SAP and Databricks Are Doing Differently

What stood out most in our session was showing how SAP and Databricks are partnering to change that. This is more than just a technical integration—it’s a strategic shift.

With SAP Business Data Cloud, customers can:

  • Access live, trusted SAP data products without duplication
  • Preserve business semantics and lineage
  • ML/AI development in pro-code environment

SAP Databricks in Business Data Cloud enables a zero-copy architecture that reduces time, effort, and risk when building production-grade AI solutions.

What Customers Told Me

One of my favorite parts of SAP Sapphire was speaking with customers one-on-one. Many were excited about how SAP-managed data products could drastically reduce the time and complexity of their data engineering tasks. The idea of getting "AI-ready" data out of SAP systems, without writing hundreds of custom pipelines, really resonated.

Several clients mentioned how this approach could help close the gap between IT and business teams, giving analysts and data scientists quicker access to meaningful, governed data. That’s a huge win for productivity and innovation.

My Takeaway

SAP Sapphire reminded me that we’re at a tipping point: data and AI are no longer "nice to have"—they’re critical to staying competitive. But what makes the difference isn’t just technology. It’s trust. Trust in the data. Trust in the platform. And trust that the tools will work together.

SAP Business Data Cloud and Databricks are helping organizations make that leap.

Let’s Keep the Conversation Going

One of the best parts of the event? Meeting new people—partners, customers, colleagues—and hearing how you're tackling data and AI challenges in the real world. Let’s not stop here.

👉 Introduce yourself in the comments! I’d love to hear what you're working on and how you see data + AI evolving at your organization.

And if you're looking to go deeper, here are some helpful resources:

  • Try it out!
  • Business Data Cloud help documentation

Let’s keep learning from each other.

Related community managed tags you should subscribe to remain informed about this topic:   

 Data and Analytics SAP Datasphere SAP Business Data Cloud

    

Related community topic pages:   

Data and Analytics  SAP Business Data Cloud  SAP Datasphere

entry.title: Deep Dive into SAP BDC Data products
entry.content:
In my earlier 2 blogs I have talked about overview of SAP BDC and its key features.

SAP BDC for Dummies - SAP Community

SAP BDC Key Features - SAP Community

In this blog we will continue to deep dive in SAP BDC key feature Data Products.

Deep Dive into SAP BDC Data products

What are Data products in SAP BDC?

Data Products in SAP Business Data Cloud serve as a standardized and efficient way to share and consume data across applications and domains. They enable analytic scenarios, AI applications, and facilitate data integration while being optimized for intensive reads. Managed with a product mindset, they are supported by high-quality metadata and governed by decentralized ownership principles.

Data Product is a grouping concept that carries the relevant metadata to describe the dataset and its data qualities. It may consists of multiple objects.

A data product is a self-contained set of tables that has data exposed for consumption outside the producing application or service via APIs.

SAP applications such as SAP S/4HANA Cloud produce data products that can be consumed in SAP Datasphere via SAP Business Data Cloud data packages. Data products are also included in and provide the data for consumption by SAP Business Data Cloud intelligent applications.

A Data Product itself is not directly consumable, but it exposes data through output ports, which can be implemented as APIs or events.

A data product provides a predefined and stable structure and also the inbound and outbound interfaces for source system extraction and end tool consumption.

Data products are created in and only available in SAP Datasphere.

Basically, a Data product acts like a Datasource that can, not-only pull data from your sources but also has the required structure to store it, load capabilities and can help consume this data across various intelligent applications.

When an Intelligent Application is installed, it often creates one or more Data Products by pulling in raw business data from source systems, transforming and organizing it during the setup process. During this process the data extraction, harmonization, and integration is taken care by Data products.

 

Key Objectives​

  1. Efficient Data Sharing Across Domains: Data Products facilitate replication-free integration and consumption by SAP, customer, and partner applications via APIs and Events.
  2. Efficient Data Storage: Data Products are processed and curated in a modern object-store based data lake architecture with highly scalable Spark processing.
  3. High-Quality Metadata: Metadata includes business semantics and is accessible through directories like the Data Product Directory and ORD Aggregators, enhancing discoverability.
  4. Optimized for Analytics: Data sets are curated for intensive reads and consumed in a read-only fashion, ensuring efficiency and reliability.
  5. Supporting Analytical and AI Applications: Data Products provide foundational data for dashboards, analytical models, and AI applications.
  6. Decentralized Ownership: Inspired by Data Mesh principles, Data Products are owned and managed by domain experts or teams responsible for operational data.
  7. Lifecycle Management: Data Products undergo needs analysis, design, and delivery phases, ensuring their accuracy and relevance.

Types

A data product can be of the type:

  • Primary: A data product exposed for consumption directly from an application. It's likely not to have input ports.
  • Derived: A data product that aggregates and combines data from multiple sources to provide value-added data sets. Derived data products must provide at least one input port with an integration dependency with at least one requirement.

Categories

Based on the dataset associated with the data product, it can have the following categories:

  • Business objects: The dataset contains business objects of type master data and transactional data.
  • Analytical data: The dataset contains analytical data such as KPIs, cubes, and metrics.
  • Other: All other types of datasets such as configuration data, mixed, and so on.

What are Data Product Definition files?

 Data Product Definition files are structured artifacts that describe and encapsulate curated datasets—either master or transactional data—designed for easy, secure, and reusable consumption across applications and domains. These files define the metadata, structure, and access interfaces of a data product.

These files are built using Open Resource Discovery (ORD) standards, ensuring consistency and interoperability.

Stored within Foundation Services, these definition files play a key role in discovering required data products.

As we continue our journey with SAP BDC we hope to discover more usage of Data products and how they can be utilized in the best way possible.

 

Happy Learning!

 

Swati Gawade-Shingade.

Cognizant technology Solutions.

entry.title: SAP Datasphere News in May
entry.content:
SAP Datasphere News in May

Another month with great new releases is in the books ✨
We delivered great new features in SAP Datasphere with all May releases. Learn more in my short top features video in May 2025 on YouTube 📺
In addition, explore the latest updates in our community news blogs.

 

My top features in May

 

Replication Flows

Reusing a Source Object in Several Replication Flows

You can now use a single source object in multiple replication flows. So far, the objects could only be used in one replication flow, but now this restriction was lifted and reusability is allowed. 
➡️  More information.

 

SFTP as Target for Replication Flow

You can now create replication flows to replicate data from supported source systems into Secure File Transfer Protocol (SFTP) targets with load types ‘initial only’ and ‘initial and delta’ with parquet, JSON and CSV files. 
➡️  More information.

 

Array support for Confluent Kafka as Source

For Confluent Kafka sources you can now expand arrays or maps when reading from Confluent topics. In the replication flow editor you can change the schema properties of an individual object with primary keys and custom opcode columns and values. 
➡️   More information.

 

Task Scheduling with shorter Increments

The new enhanced scheduling feature allows tasks to be set at 5-minute intervals, offering greater flexibility and precision in task management. 
➡️   More information.

 

Local Table Partitioning With Hash Partitioning

When creating your local table from the Data Builder you can define partitions based on one or several columns of your table to break your data down into smaller and more manageable parts. You can now choose between range partitioning or hash partitioning. 
➡️   More information.

 

 

SAPPHIRE

May covered two exceptional SAP SAPPHIRE events in Orlando and Madrid. We had great coverage with more than 30 sessions on SAP Business Data Cloud including SAP Datasphere by product managers, executives, customers, and partners covering all areas of BDC including roadmap sessions, demos, and strategy talks. Some of them have been recorded and can be accessed via the 🔗 SAP Business Data Cloud track on the session catalog.

 

More blogs to check out 👇

  • Export Datasphere Views Data in CSV Format
  • Release Navigator for SAP BTP - What's New in Q2 2025
  • SAP BDC Key Features
  • New Webinar Series: Architecting Your SAP Business Data Cloud Journey – From Data to Intelligence!
  • SAP Business Data CloudについてのFAQ
  • SAP BDC for Dummies
  • SAP Datasphere CLI & Python Automation : ADD 1000+ Users in Datasphere space in few seconds
  • 3 different ways to log in Datasphere using SSO
  • SAP Databricks in SAP Business Data Cloud : Unifying SAP Business Data with Lakehouse Intelligence
  • Designing a Lightweight Sales Forecasting Prototype in SAP Datasphere with ChatGPT and Python
  • SAP Sapphire Orlando 2025: SAP and Databricks open a bold new era of data and AI - BDC2767
  • Become an Early Adopter for Joule in SAP Datasphere
  • From Vision to Reality: Enabling Data Mesh with SAP Business Data Cloud
  • SAP Business Data Cloud – Central catalog feature revealed
  • Integrating Open SQL Schema Tables with SAP Datasphere Space using Dataflow
  • Monitoring the resource consumption of seamless planning in SAP Datasphere
  • Sapphire Orlando&Madrid 2025: World champion analytics and performance in esports with SAP software
  • SAP Datasphere CLI & Python Automation : Extract All Artifacts of Datasphere in CSV files.
  • Automizing authentication with SAP Datasphere CLI via OAuth 2.0
  • Consuming complex Datasphere models

 

Find more information and related blog posts on the topic page for SAP Datasphere. You will find further product information on our Community with various subpages about Business Content, the SAP BW Bridge as well as content for Best Practices & Troubleshooting. Also check out the new support content for SAP Datasphere on SAP Help for troubleshooting and analysis guides, how-to guides, technical details, and more.

Find out how to unleash the power of your business data with SAP’s free learning content on SAP Datasphere. It’s designed to help you enrich your data projects, simplify the data landscape, and make the most out of your investment. Check out even more role-based learning resources and opportunities to get certified in one place on  SAP Learning site.

entry.title: Understanding ABAP Pipeline Engine used for Data Integration in Datasphere
entry.content:
Background:

This article would help in understanding how ABAP Pipeline engine aids data extraction process when you try to extract data from any S/4 HANA Table or a S/4 HANA CDS view which is Extraction and CDC Enabled CDS using a Replication flow of Datasphere with Initial Only and Initial + Delta options.

A Data-integration would be required to ingest the data from Source to a Target where an Advanced Analytics or Modelling or reporting would be performed.

In Data-extraction, there are two aspects

  • Full load (Initial Load only; One-off load)
  • Initial Load +Delta load.

Full Load: It is also called as Initial Load only or One-off load, where in the load will happen only once; post the load the delta records will not be flowing to the target.

Initial + Delta: It will first do a full load, post that the deltas will be pushed to the target based on the specified frequency.

How  SLT works:  Whenever we keep any table for replication it creates

  • Log Table – A table is one which is meant to hold the delta records
  • Trigger – Insert update and Delete triggers were created on top the base table in source; whenever any CRUD operation happened, respective triggers were invoked which in-turn ingests the data into the log table; based on the frequency set, these records from the log table were pushed to the target.

Similarly; in Datasphere, it uses ABAP Pipeline Engines (APE) which aids in achieving the two aspects of Data Integration mentioned above.

Either a direct table or an Extraction, CDC enabled CDS view can be used for data extraction. In the either of the cases the behavior is same.

Below screenshot from a standard CDS view I_CUSTOMER, in the CDC Mapping annotation, KNA1 is the primary table from which the delta is to be deduced.

In nutshell, if we keep either KNA1 table for replication or I_CUSTOMER for replication(which is based on KNA1) the behavior of APE will the same and both the cases the delta will be deduced with respect to KNA1.

ABAP Pipeline Engine(APE): Used for integrating and processing data from ABAP-based systems, such as SAP S/4HANA, within broader data workflows.

In this blog we shall see what happens when we use a Extraction CDC enabled CDS view for extraction generates below components of APE.

Scenario 1: Initial Load + Delta
When we extract an Extraction and CDC enabled CDS view using Replication flow of Datasphere, below components are generated.

  • Trigger creation : Whenever any table or CDS view is kept for replication, it creates Insert, Update and Delete triggers on the top of the table (or table which is defined in cdc mapping of  CDS view). Respective triggers are invoked in case of any insert, update or delete operations on the table.
  • Master Logging table: The master logging table is the single source of truth for this job to catch the triggers, i.e  its the first table which gets filled in once any of the triggers are triggered because of a CRUD operation.         
    • Naming convention-  /1DH/ML<XXXXXXXXX>     
    • Data Definition of a Master Logging Table:

  • Number of Master Logging table created is equal number of CDC mappings defined in the CDS view
  • In the below example two master Logging tables are created

If we check in DHCDCMON t-code, we can see that two Master and Subscriber logging tables are created

  • Subscriber Logging table:
    • Once there are records filled in the Master Logging table, a real time job /1DH/OBSERVE_LOGTAB is triggered, which ingests the data into the Subscriber logging table
    • Naming Convention- Same name as Master logging table. M replaced with S /1DH/SL<XXXXXXXXX>.
    • Data Definition
      • The structure is same as Master Logging table, but the key here are the key fields of the base table (on which the cdc mapping is defined)

  • Buffer Table:
    • DHRDBMON is the tcode in which we can see the buffer table name for the corresponding CDS view name.
    • Naming Convention /1DH/RDB<XXXXX>
    • Data Definition: This has same structure as the CDS view which we have to extract the data.
    • /1DH/SEQUENCE is the key
    • Once the records are ingested in the Subscriber logging table, real time job /1DH/CDS_PUSH_DELTA  is triggered, which ingests the records into the Buffer table by making a join back to the base table based on the keys and ingest the records in the buffer table which has as definition of CDS view.

  • Real Time Jobs:
    • /1DH/OBSERVE_LOGTAB is a standard real time job which gets auto triggered whenever there are records in Master Logging table. It reschedules itself for every 1hour (Default setting) to avoid coming into the list of long running jobs
    • /1DH/CDS_PUSH_DELTA is a standard real time job which is auto triggered whenever there are records in Subscriber Logging table. . It reschedules itself for every 6hours (Default setting) to avoid coming into the list of long running jobs
    • They can been seen in SM37, they run under DDIC user.

Example: Lets consider a Custom CDS view with Definition

Advantages:

We can create multiple Extraction CDC enabled CDS views with same source; when extracting data from these two CDS views using Datasphere with INIT + Delta option, the Trigger, Master and Subscriber logging table are common with subscribers. i.e two different buffer table will be created for the respective CDS views based on the fields that are selected in the CDS view projection.

Subscriber 1 and Subscriber 2: Refer to the two Extraction CDC Enabled CDS Views that are built using the same source and is kept for data extraction using Replication flow of Datasphere.

Scenario 2: Initial Load Only:

Whenever we extract data from a CDS view with INIT Only option in Datasphere, below artifacts are created.

A buffer table is created based on the structure of the CDS view used in Replication flow of Datasphere, from which the data is read by Datasphere.

Summary:

 

FeatureMaster Logging TableSubscriber Logging TableBuffer Table
Naming Convention/1DH/ML<XXXXXXXXX>   /1DH/SL<XXXXXXXXX>  /1DH/RDB<XXXXX>
Key/1DH/SEQUENCEKey of the base table/1DH/SEQUENCE 
Data FillGets filled from TriggersGets filled in once /1DH/LOGTAB is triggeredGets filled in once /1DH/CDS_PUSH_DELTA is triggered
Table MaintanenceRecords are deleted once the Subscriber logging table is filledRecords are delted once the Buffer table is filledRecords are deleted once the data is ingested to target
Table StructureHas the key fields of the base tableHas key fields of the base tableHas all the fields of the CDS view
TcodeDHCDCMONDHCDCMONDHRDBMON
Read by Datasphere  Datasphere accesses this table to ingest the data to target
Table GenrationOne of each table defined in CDC MappingOne of each table defined in CDC Mappingone per Extraction Enabled CDS view
Table GenrationNumber of Master logging table is equal
to the number CDC mapping defined in the CDS view		Number of Subscriber logging table is equal to the number CDC
 mapping defined in the CDS view		One Extraction Enabled CDS view is equal to one Buffer table
ScopeComes into picture only for INIT+Delta Comes into picture only for INIT+Delta Comes into picture for both Initial only and INIT+Delta
